data.title: Foreign ownership of long-term U.S. Treasuries hits record high, Apollo warns
data.description:
Foreign investors now hold a record share of long-dated U.S. Treasuries, raising concerns about increased sensitivity to rising interest rates, according to Apollo’s chief economist, Torsten Sløk.

Sløk noted that international investors have steadily increased their exposure to U.S. government bonds with maturities exceeding 10 years. This shift, he explained, stems from the past decade’s ultra-low and often negative interest rate environments in major economies such as Europe and Japan. With U.S. Treasuries offering comparatively higher nominal yields, global investors found them increasingly attractive.

However, Sløk cautioned that this growing concentration in long-duration assets may now pose risks. As long-term interest rates continue to rise, the value of these holdings becomes more volatile, potentially exposing foreign portfolios to sizable mark-to-market losses. 

The surge in foreign demand for long-term U.S. debt highlights the global reach of American monetary policy and the ripple effects of shifting yield curves. 

See the below chart provided by Apollo:

Treasury and Fixed Income ETFs: (NASDAQ:TLT), (NYSEARCA:TLH), (NASDAQ:IEF), (NASDAQ:IEI), (NASDAQ:SHY), (NYSEARCA:SGOV), (NYSEARCA:SCHO), (NYSEARCA:BIL), (NYSEARCA:AGG), (NASDAQ:BND), (NASDAQ:VCIT), (NYSEARCA:MUB), (NASDAQ:MBB), (NYSEARCA:JNK), (NYSEARCA:LQD), (NYSEARCA:HYG), and (NYSEARCA:TIP).

data.title: UroGen continues to climb after bladder cancer drug approval
data.description:
• After closing Thursday up ~52% following the U.S. FDA's approval of its bladder cancer drug Zusduri (mitomycin), UroGen Pharma (NASDAQ:URGN) is up ~10% in Friday morning trading.
  • Zusduri is approved for recurrent low-grade intermediate-risk non-muscle invasive bladder cancer.
  • On Friday, Guggenheim doubled its price target to $30 (~171% upside based on Thursday's close) and maintained its buy rating.

data.title: Investors flock to gold once again as a classic safe haven amid Middle East war escalations
data.description:
Gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) rose alongside the US dollar (DXY) on Friday after news of the Israeli attack on Iran as a “classic safe-haven move.”

“The current upswing is largely driven by geopolitical risks and investment demand for tangible hard assets – particularly for precious metals,” said Ole Hansen, head of commodity strategy at Saxo Bank.

The combination of rising fiscal debt concerns, tariff-driven supply shocks, and continued U.S. dollar (DXY) weakness has created conditions where “the groundwork for a potential push toward USD 4,000 cannot be ruled out” for gold, he said. The gold spot price (XAUUSD:CUR) is currently $3,434, up 30% year-to-date, and rising more than 1.4% on Friday.

 

 

 

This rise is also lifting the commodities sector, which is headed for its second consecutive weekly gain, with the Bloomberg Commodity Total Return Index (DJP) up 3.3% this month and year-to-date returns in U.S. dollar exceeding 9%.

This performance significantly outpaced other dollar-denominated assets including bonds (SPLB), (GBF), (BND), the S&P 500 (SP500), and Nasdaq (COMP:IND).

“This week’s gain was primarily due to haven demand for gold (NYSEARCA:GLD) and a surging risk premium in crude oil (CL1:COM) in response to heightened geopolitical tensions following Israel’s latest attack on Iran,” said Hansen.

In addition, energy prices have rebounded dramatically this month, with Brent Crude (CL1:COM) spiking as much as 13% to reach USD 78.50 per barrel after Israel launched airstrikes on Iranian nuclear and ballistic missile facilities.

The escalation has once again raised concerns about potential supply disruptions in a region responsible for a third of global oil output.

Industrial metals have remained mostly flat this month, with gains in aluminum (LMAHDS03:COM) offset by weakness in other segments, particularly copper (HG1:COM), (CPER). Despite a slight pullback, copper remains up approximately 18.7% year-to-date, supported by a wide price spread between U.S. and international markets.

“This price dislocation has prompted traders to ship metal to the U.S. ahead of such tariff announcements, thereby draining exchange-monitored stockpiles in London and Shanghai,” creating supply tightness even as demand faces pressure from slowing global growth, said Hansen.

The agricultural sector continues to struggle with broad losses across key crops, despite pockets of strength in coffee and cattle. Hansens pointed to “ample supply despite an increasingly volatile weather situation across the world” as the primary factor pressuring agricultural commodities, while noting that “the U.S. meat market where import restrictions and a small herd have underpinned prices in recent months” remains a rare bright spot in the sector.

data.title: Israel-Iran tensions hit travel stocks
data.description:
Heightened geopolitical tensions fueled by Israel's airstrike on Iran are weighing on travel-related stocks, with airline, cruise, and hotel shares all trading in the red.

Airline stocks are bearing the brunt of the selloff, particularly carriers with significant exposure to international routes. Shares of United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL), Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL), and American Airlines Group (NASDAQ:AAL) are underperforming the sector, each down 4% to 5%, while the U.S. Global Jets ETF (NYSEARCA:JETS) is off 3%.

Cruise stocks are also in the red led by a ~5% drop in shares of Carnival Corporation (NYSE:CCL) whose Cunard line operates itineraries in the Mediterranean and Western Africa. Royal Caribbean (NYSE:RCL), Norwegian Cruise Lines (NYSE:NCLH), and Viking Holdings (NYSE:VIK) are also trading defensively.

Losses have spilled into hotel stocks, as well as online travel platforms including Expedia (NASDAQ:EXPE), Booking Holdings (NASDAQ:BKNG), Tripadvisor (NASDAQ:TRIP), Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB), and Travelzoo (NASDAQ:TZOO).

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click  here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: AstraZeneca signs research agreement with China's CSPC
data.description:
• AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN) said on Friday that it has formed a research partnership with CSPC Pharmaceuticals, located in Shijiazhuang City, China. 
  • The collaboration focuses on identifying and developing pre-clinical drug candidates for various chronic diseases, which includes a small molecule oral therapy targeting immunological conditions.
  • As part of the deal, CSPC will receive an initial payment of $110M, with the potential to earn up to $1.62B in development milestones and an additional $3.6B in sales milestones.
  • CSPC may also earn royalties based on the annual net sales of the products.
  • Additionally, AstraZeneca said it will maintain the rights to secure exclusive licenses for the development and commercialization of any candidates discovered during this partnership.

data.title: Consumer sentiment perks up in June as inflation expectations ease
data.description:
June Consumer Sentiment: 60.5 vs. 53.5 consensus and 52.2 prior.

One-year inflation expectations: 5.1% vs. 6.6% prior.

Five-year implied inflation: 4.1% vs. 4.2% prior.

Current conditions: 63.7 vs. 59.4 consensus and 58.9 prior.

Consumer expectations: 58.4 vs. 49.0 consensus and 47.9 prior.

Developing... check back for updates.

data.title: Moderna under pressure despite RSV vaccine approval into wider age group
data.description:
• Moderna (NASDAQ:MRNA) shares are down ~2% in early Friday trading despite the U.S. FDA approving the company's RSV shot RSV, mRESVIA (mRNA-1345), in an expanded population.
  • Although the approval is a positive for the company, it could face an uphill climb that is critical to its commercial success: Getting the CDC's Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices to back its expanded use.
  • While mRESVIA expanded indication covers at-risk adults aged 18 to 59, payors may not cover the vaccine without an ACIP endorsement in this population. 
  • That could be a tall order given that HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., a vaccine skeptic, this week first removed all members of that panel and then replaced them with eight individuals of his own choosing, without any outside vetting. 
  • Some of the eight have been critical of COVID vaccines and social restrictions implemented during the pandemic.

data.title: Ripple CEO says XRP could capture 14% of SWIFT volume by 2030
data.description:
The XRP (XRP-USD) cryptocurrency could capture some 14% of the cross-border payment volume currently handled by global messaging network SWIFT within five years, Ripple CEO Brad Garlinghouse earlier in this week.

Garlinghouse tied his projection to XRP's (XRP-USD) role as a blockchain-powered liquidity tool for global fund transfers, going beyond a messaging system. SWIFT is used by banks to send payment instructions, but it doesn't actually move the money itself.

“There are two parts to SWIFT today: messaging and liquidity,” Garlinghouse said at the XRP APEX 2025 event in Singapore on Wednesday. “Liquidity is owned by the banks. I think less about the messaging and more about liquidity. If you're driving all the liquidity, it is good for XRP … so I’ll say five years, 14%.”

As a bridge currency, XRP (XRP-USD) facilitates instant exchanges between currencies, cutting down the need for traditional banks to park money abroad.

The price of XRP (XRP-USD) slid 7.5% so far this year, but surged ~350% on a year-over-year basis.

data.title: Tech stocks follow broader market down slightly after Israel strikes Iran
data.description:
An array of tech stocks, including those in the Magnificent Seven, trended down as markets opened on Friday, mimicking the slight declines seen across the major indices, as conflict increases in the Middle East.

The iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (BATS:IGV) had slipped 1%. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) had declined 2%.

Most of the tech names in the Magnificent Seven followed suit. Google (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL) and Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) had both declined 1%, Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) slid 1.4%, Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) inched down 0.3% and Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) dropped marginally by 0.1%. Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) proved to be the outlier, edging up 0.4%.

Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR), a software company specializing in artificial intelligence and data analytics, has a partnership with the Israeli Defense Ministry. Its shares had climbed 0.9%. 

Monday.com (NASDAQ:MNDY), an Israel-based work management solutions company, had dropped 3%.

Most semiconductor firms were demonstrating declines. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM) was down 1.9%. Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) had slipped 0.7%. Qualcomm (NASDAQ:QCOM) fell 1.3%. Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL) slid 1.7%.

IBM (NYSE:IBM) and Kyndryl Holdings (NYSE:KD) both inched down 1%.

These slight declines are similar to what the broader market was showing. The S&P 500 (SP500) was down 0.6%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) slid 0.7% and the Dow (DJI) declined 1%.

"The effects of the attack have cascaded across global markets, with a strong risk-off move for several asset classes," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

Late Thursday, Israel targeted Iran with a series of strikes against nuclear sites and military targets, raising fears of an all-out war in the Middle East. Iran also launched over 100 drones at Israel overnight in retaliation to Israel's "pre-emptive" strikes. Israeli airspace has been closed, and a state of emergency has been declared as a counterattack was anticipated.

"This operation will continue for as many days as it takes to remove this threat," said Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.

data.title: Omnicom, Paramount Global most shorted S&P 500 communications stocks in May; Alphabet least shorted
data.description:
Bets against communication services stocks fell in May compared to the end of April, with Omnicom Group (OMC) and Paramount Global (PARA) being the most shorted stock in the sector and Google-parent Alphabet (GOOG) taking the spot for the least shorted.

Average short interest across S&P 500 communications services stocks was 1.75% of shares float in end-May, down from 1.87% at the end of April.

The S&P 500’s communications services sector (NYSEARCA:XLC) is up over 7% so far this year, compared to the 2.7% rise in the broader market.

Stocks with the largest and least short positions

Ranked by short interest as a percentage of shares float

Meta Platforms Class A (META), Alphabet Class A (GOOGL) and Netflix (NFLX), the three largest contributors to the index, had short interest of 1.43%, 1.14% and 1.85%, respectively.

Omnicom Group (OMC) had 25.98 million shares sold short as of May 31, or 13.48% of shares float, while Paramount Global (PARA) had 80.51 million shares sold.

Charter Communications (CHTR) was the second most shorted stock at 12.21% of shares float, followed by Live Nation Entertainment (LYV) and TKO Group Holdings (TKO), with short interest of 1.88% and 8.15%, respectively.

Alphabet (GOOG) and Walt Disney (DIS), were the least shorted stocks, which had 31.94 million and 17.78 million shares sold.

Industry Analysis

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

Media was the most shorted industry within the sector, with 3.74% short interest as of end-May, up from 3.26% during end-April.

Entertainment stocks took the second spot with 3.22% short interest as of May, compared to 2.88% at April-end.

Interactive Media and Services, which has stocks like Alphabet and Meta Platforms, was the least shorted industry in the sector with 0.55% short interest as of May.
